EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large weather system impacted the region on May 6th and brought a few rounds of rain and storms to the area. A few instances of severe weather occurred across the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down east of Kokomo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines and poles were downed and snapped associated with the rear flank downdraft of the Thaxton/New Albany tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large weather system impacted the region on May 6th and brought a few rounds of rain and storms to the area. A few instances of severe weather occurred across the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down in Columbus and around the county by strong winds associated with a wake low. Two homes were damaged by fallen trees. A peak wind gust of 51 mph out of the east was measured by the Golden Triangle Airport AWOS site KGTR during this time frame.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few isolated supercell storms developed during the afternoon of May 8th. One cell intensified and produced a swath of significant hail along with wind damage on the north side of Starkville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic snowstorm transformed the northern Gulf Coast into a winter wonderland on January 21, 2025. This snowstorm shattered longstanding snowfall records across the region, including the nearly 130-year-old snowfall records that were set during the February Snowstorm of 1895. Up to 5 inches of snow was observed, with the highest totals along the southern portions of George and Stone Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 and 5 inches of snow fell across the county, with the heaviest totals focused in the southern portions.

A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Davis Weather Station measured an 85 mph gust in the Philipp Community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across portions of Mississippi during the afternoon of May 25th before a west-east oriented squall line developed and spread southward overnight. Another squall line moved through on the 26th as a cold front moved east. Storms produced wind damage and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down between Winona and Duck Hill.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado started just west of Haynes Chapel Road and ended just after it moved east across Lake Road. A tornadic debris signature was noted on radar with this tornado, and several trees were uprooted or snapped as it crossed Haynes Chapel Road. Other minor tree damage was noted at Lake Road. Maximum estimated wind speeds were 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across portions of Mississippi during the afternoon of May 25th before a west-east oriented squall line developed and spread southward overnight. Another squall line moved through on the 26th as a cold front moved east. Storms produced wind damage and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down just north of Enterprise and tracked east-northeast through the Basic community and dissipated just east of Highway 45 in far southern Lauderdale County. Scattered trees were broken and uprooted along the path. Most of the tree damage was along county road 379 and 350. The maximum estimated wind speeds in Clarke County were 90 mph. The total path length was 8.77 miles across the two counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat dome continued to strengthen and expand across the eastern CONUS during late June. The upper level pattern was dominated by a large 594 dam Bermuda High over the eastern US with a deep trough over the West Coast. This pattern persisted for several days before the ridge finally broke down and the westerlies advanced further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system moved out of central Arkansas and into the Mid-South during the mid-morning hours on June 7, 2025. The system re-intensified as it moved into an airmass that was favorable for severe storms. Moderate to strong instability, moderately steep mid-level lapse rates, and 0-6 km bulk shear values more than 40 kts helped support severe storms resulting in damaging winds, and a weak tornado. Storms cleared out of the Mid-South by early afternoon, and the atmosphere was able to recover and become unstable once again. An upper-level disturbance moving through during the evening hours produced another round of storms, some of them severe, with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on the Antioch Baptist Church.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dewpoints of 70 degrees played a significant role in reducing convective inhibition across the Mid-South, as well as enhancing low-level CAPE for storm initiation. 0-6km shear was not particularly strong, generally less than 25 knots, but increased to around 35 kts with a passing shortwave. Storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported trees knocked down  in the Waterford, MS area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse-type showers and thunderstorms continued during the afternoon hours with torrential rainfall rates. Due to a lack of shear, slow moving/nearly stationary storm motion heightened flash flooding threats, particularly over urban areas. A storm developed over Tupelo, MS and dumped buckets of rain over the area resulting in widespread flash flooding. The airport recorded 3.43 inches and set a daily rainfall record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coley Road, just west of the airport, was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant multi-hazard, multi-day event occurred across the Mid-South from April 2, 2025, to April 8, 2025, producing 35 tornadoes, record flooding, and numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail.  A large upper-level trough covered the Western U.S. in early April. A significant piece of energy rotated around the base of the trough and ejected into the Southern Plains and the Middle-Upper Mississippi Valley on April 2nd. A 500 mb jet maximum of 120 knots and a 300 mb jet maximum of 140 knots pushed into Iowa by late afternoon on April 2nd.  Meanwhile, a 992 mb surface low moved into the Upper Mississippi Valley with a trailing cold front pushing toward the Mid-South. A secondary, weaker surface low developed over northeast Arkansas and helped to back surface winds. The warm sector across the Mid-South was potent with surface dewpoints climbing into the upper 60s and MLCAPE values climbing to 2000-3000 J/kg. Increasing upper-level divergence occurred in the entrance region of the upper jet, resulting in strong lift across the Mid-South and storm initiation in the increasingly moist and unstable airmass. Hodographs were long, strongly curved, and supportive of tornadoes. Discrete supercells intensified across the Mid-South during the evening as 0-6 km bulk shear values increased to 60-70 knots, and 0-1 km helicity values increased to 300-400 m2/s2. Fifteen tornadoes occurred from late afternoon on April 2nd through about 2 am on April 3rd, including four EF-3s. This period represented the most significant period of severe weather during the event. ||The cold front stalled across the Mid-South on April 3rd while moist southwesterly flow aloft continued and anomalous precipitable water values prevailed across the region. Heavy rain fell across northern sections of the Mid-South into the morning hours of April 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 3-4 inches were common across east-central Arkansas, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi, along with scattered instances of flash flooding. By the afternoon of April 3rd, the airmass south of the stalled front destabilized and storms strengthened thanks to a 90-knot mid-level jet maximum moving through Missouri. These storms produced wind damage, large hail, flash flooding, and a tornado east of Corinth. Heavy rain continued into the early morning hours on April 4th when the front eventually lifted north of the area, resulting in a relative lull during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on Dorse Rd in Michigan City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado entered southern Tallahatchie County where it destroyed a mobile home along Forty Mile Bend Rd and snapped and uprooted several trees.  The tornado then crossed the Tallahatchie River into Leflore County. Estimated peak wind gusts were 110 mph.

The tornado began to intensify along Brownsferry Road, snapping trunks of several trees. Further intensification continued where the worst of the damage occurred near Waverly Circle. One home had multiple walls collapsed, and another home had all of its walls collapsed onto the concrete slab. The One- or Two-Family Residence damage indicator indicated high-end EF3 damage consistent with wind speeds around 160 mph, given the materials used to fasten walls to the foundation. The tornado continued to track northeast and crossed Highway 4, west of Senatobia, where more tree trunks were snapped and several light poles were damaged. The roof of a home and additional trees were damaged near the intersection of Veazey Road and Stage Road. The tornado then crossed Highway 51 and Interstate 55, causing more tree damage. The tornado caused additional damage to a neighborhood near Rifle Range Road, damaging the roofs of homes and causing tree damage from David Road through Peyton Road. The tornado finally lifted just before crossing Billy's Creek. Estimated peak winds were around 165 mph.

The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 61 between Church Road and Star Landing Road was flooded. Blythe Rd was also closed, and a water rescue was conducted in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant multi-hazard, multi-day event occurred across the Mid-South from April 2, 2025, to April 8, 2025, producing 35 tornadoes, record flooding, and numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail.  A large upper-level trough covered the Western U.S. in early April. A significant piece of energy rotated around the base of the trough and ejected into the Southern Plains and the Middle-Upper Mississippi Valley on April 2nd. A 500 mb jet maximum of 120 knots and a 300 mb jet maximum of 140 knots pushed into Iowa by late afternoon on April 2nd.  Meanwhile, a 992 mb surface low moved into the Upper Mississippi Valley with a trailing cold front pushing toward the Mid-South. A secondary, weaker surface low developed over northeast Arkansas and helped to back surface winds. The warm sector across the Mid-South was potent with surface dewpoints climbing into the upper 60s and MLCAPE values climbing to 2000-3000 J/kg. Increasing upper-level divergence occurred in the entrance region of the upper jet, resulting in strong lift across the Mid-South and storm initiation in the increasingly moist and unstable airmass. Hodographs were long, strongly curved, and supportive of tornadoes. Discrete supercells intensified across the Mid-South during the evening as 0-6 km bulk shear values increased to 60-70 knots, and 0-1 km helicity values increased to 300-400 m2/s2. Fifteen tornadoes occurred from late afternoon on April 2nd through about 2 am on April 3rd, including four EF-3s. This period represented the most significant period of severe weather during the event. ||The cold front stalled across the Mid-South on April 3rd while moist southwesterly flow aloft continued and anomalous precipitable water values prevailed across the region. Heavy rain fell across northern sections of the Mid-South into the morning hours of April 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 3-4 inches were common across east-central Arkansas, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi, along with scattered instances of flash flooding. By the afternoon of April 3rd, the airmass south of the stalled front destabilized and storms strengthened thanks to a 90-knot mid-level jet maximum moving through Missouri. These storms produced wind damage, large hail, flash flooding, and a tornado east of Corinth. Heavy rain continued into the early morning hours on April 4th when the front eventually lifted north of the area, resulting in a relative lull during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted associated with the rear flank downdraft of the Senatobia tornado track.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system moved out of central Arkansas and into the Mid-South during the mid-morning hours on June 7, 2025. The system re-intensified as it moved into an airmass that was favorable for severe storms. Moderate to strong instability, moderately steep mid-level lapse rates, and 0-6 km bulk shear values more than 40 kts helped support severe storms resulting in damaging winds, and a weak tornado. Storms cleared out of the Mid-South by early afternoon, and the atmosphere was able to recover and become unstable once again. An upper-level disturbance moving through during the evening hours produced another round of storms, some of them severe, with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down and blocked the road at Twin Lakes Road and Highway 41.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dewpoints of 70 degrees played a significant role in reducing convective inhibition across the Mid-South, as well as enhancing low-level CAPE for storm initiation. 0-6km shear was not particularly strong, generally less than 25 knots, but increased to around 35 kts with a passing shortwave. Storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported tree blocking road at intersection of CR 100 and CR 157.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse-type showers and thunderstorms continued during the afternoon hours with torrential rainfall rates. Due to a lack of shear, slow moving/nearly stationary storm motion heightened flash flooding threats, particularly over urban areas. A storm developed over Tupelo, MS and dumped buckets of rain over the area resulting in widespread flash flooding. The airport recorded 3.43 inches and set a daily rainfall record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ruffwood Road and nearby roadways were impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitable water values remain high along and east of the Mississippi River, around 2 inches. Isolated slow-moving storms produced localized heavy rainfall and a couple of instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred at Burnsville Baseball Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitable water values remain high along and east of the Mississippi River, around 2 inches. Isolated slow-moving storms produced localized heavy rainfall and a couple of instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along Hwy 172 in the community of Oldham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first heat wave of 2025 started on June 20, 2025, as an upper-level ridge amplified into the Lower Mississippi Valley. High temperatures climbed into the 90s areawide, with the highest heat indices focused along the Mississippi River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS reported a maximum heat index of 109 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were uprooted associated with the rear flank downdraft of the Thaxton/New Albany tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent southerly winds under a building upper-level ridge pushed air temperatures into the mid 90s along with oppressive humidity. Heat indices peaked in the range of 105 to 109 degrees during the afternoon hours. Afternoon convective coverage over performed, and portions of north Mississippi were spared from the oppressive heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS reported a maximum heat index of 107 degrees.

An upper-level disturbance moving through during the evening hours produced another round of storms, some of them severe, with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down in northwestern Chickasaw County, MS, off County Road 2. Sporadic tree damage was noted as the tornado tracked southeastward to a farm with several outbuildings and a barn with structural damage. The most significant damage occurred at a large barn at HWY 32W and CR 3, where major sections of the metal roof were uplifted and lofted several hundred yards into the field next to it. A couple of empty grain bins sustained minor damage, and several vehicles and farm equipment had windows blown out due to flying debris. The tornado followed HWY 32W toward CR 6 and snapped more large tree branches, as well as lofting a small carport. As the tornado continued southeastward along HWY 32W, minor tree damage was evident almost to New Houlka. The tornado lifted shortly before reaching the west side|of town.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near Highway 311, southwest of Slayden, uprooting trees and snapping trunks near Drew Road. As the tornado moved northeast, additional tree damage and some roof damage to a home occurred along Isom Chapel Road. The tornado began to intensify, causing all exterior walls of a home to collapse along Hurdle Club Road in addition to the continuation of uprooting trees. Uprooted trees and snapped tree trunks were widespread across Hogan Road before causing another home's exterior walls to collapse along South Slayden Road. The tornado continued northeast across Marshall County before crossing Highway 72 on the east side of Slayden. Near Kennedy Road, the metal trusses supporting high-tension power lines and a home���s exterior walls collapsed, as well as widespread, significant tree damage. Continuing northeast, Early Grove Road had additional tree damage, and another home was damaged. Just before crossing the county line into Benton County, Rice Chapel Road had widespread tree damage with snapped tree trunks. Estimated peak winds were around 160 mph.

The tornado continued through a wooded area approaching Hwy 72, crossing just west of the Glen Town Hall. The Glendale Elementary School sustained minor damage to the roof (less than 20%) and several trees were uprooted here. The tornado weakened after this point and sporadic tree damage was noted throughout a wooded area to the northeast between County Road 248 and County Road 260 before crossing into Tishomingo. Peak winds were estimated to be around 90 mph.

The system re-intensified as it moved into an airmass that was favorable for severe storms. Moderate to strong instability, moderately steep mid-level lapse rates, and 0-6 km bulk shear values more than 40 kts helped support severe storms resulting in damaging winds, and a weak tornado. Storms cleared out of the Mid-South by early afternoon, and the atmosphere was able to recover and become unstable once again. An upper-level disturbance moving through during the evening hours produced another round of storms, some of them severe, with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down across McComb Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat dome continued to strengthen and expand across the eastern CONUS during late June. The upper level pattern was dominated by a large 594 dam Bermuda High over the eastern US with a deep trough over the West Coast. This pattern persisted for several days before the ridge finally broke down and the westerlies advanced further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado crossed into northwest Pontotoc County, causing significant damage to trees and utility poles northwest of Thaxton, especially along Highway 336 and Carter Road. As the tornado moved northeast toward Hurricane, it produced substantial damage to several farm outbuildings and removed part of a roof from a home on Wells Road. Additional tree and utility pole damage was noted along Todd Road and Highways 346 and 355. The storm also had a substantial rear flank downdraft, resulting in straight-line wind damage in the Hurricane community east of the tornado track. The tornado then moved over forested bottomland around Mud Creek before crossing into Union County. Estimated peak winds across Pontotoc were 120 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant multi-hazard, multi-day event occurred across the Mid-South from April 2, 2025, to April 8, 2025, producing 35 tornadoes, record flooding, and numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail.  A large upper-level trough covered the Western U.S. in early April. A significant piece of energy rotated around the base of the trough and ejected into the Southern Plains and the Middle-Upper Mississippi Valley on April 2nd. A 500 mb jet maximum of 120 knots and a 300 mb jet maximum of 140 knots pushed into Iowa by late afternoon on April 2nd.  Meanwhile, a 992 mb surface low moved into the Upper Mississippi Valley with a trailing cold front pushing toward the Mid-South. A secondary, weaker surface low developed over northeast Arkansas and helped to back surface winds. The warm sector across the Mid-South was potent with surface dewpoints climbing into the upper 60s and MLCAPE values climbing to 2000-3000 J/kg. Increasing upper-level divergence occurred in the entrance region of the upper jet, resulting in strong lift across the Mid-South and storm initiation in the increasingly moist and unstable airmass. Hodographs were long, strongly curved, and supportive of tornadoes. Discrete supercells intensified across the Mid-South during the evening as 0-6 km bulk shear values increased to 60-70 knots, and 0-1 km helicity values increased to 300-400 m2/s2. Fifteen tornadoes occurred from late afternoon on April 2nd through about 2 am on April 3rd, including four EF-3s. This period represented the most significant period of severe weather during the event. ||The cold front stalled across the Mid-South on April 3rd while moist southwesterly flow aloft continued and anomalous precipitable water values prevailed across the region. Heavy rain fell across northern sections of the Mid-South into the morning hours of April 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 3-4 inches were common across east-central Arkansas, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi, along with scattered instances of flash flooding. By the afternoon of April 3rd, the airmass south of the stalled front destabilized and storms strengthened thanks to a 90-knot mid-level jet maximum moving through Missouri. These storms produced wind damage, large hail, flash flooding, and a tornado east of Corinth. Heavy rain continued into the early morning hours on April 4th when the front eventually lifted north of the area, resulting in a relative lull during the day on April 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant multi-hazard, multi-day event occurred across the Mid-South from April 2, 2025, to April 8, 2025, producing 35 tornadoes, record flooding, and numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail.  A large upper-level trough covered the Western U.S. in early April. A significant piece of energy rotated around the base of the trough and ejected into the Southern Plains and the Middle-Upper Mississippi Valley on April 2nd. A 500 mb jet maximum of 120 knots and a 300 mb jet maximum of 140 knots pushed into Iowa by late afternoon on April 2nd.  Meanwhile, a 992 mb surface low moved into the Upper Mississippi Valley with a trailing cold front pushing toward the Mid-South. A secondary, weaker surface low developed over northeast Arkansas and helped to back surface winds. The warm sector across the Mid-South was potent with surface dewpoints climbing into the upper 60s and MLCAPE values climbing to 2000-3000 J/kg. Increasing upper-level divergence occurred in the entrance region of the upper jet, resulting in strong lift across the Mid-South and storm initiation in the increasingly moist and unstable airmass. Hodographs were long, strongly curved, and supportive of tornadoes. Discrete supercells intensified across the Mid-South during the evening as 0-6 km bulk shear values increased to 60-70 knots, and 0-1 km helicity values increased to 300-400 m2/s2. Fifteen tornadoes occurred from late afternoon on April 2nd through about 2 am on April 3rd, including four EF-3s. This period represented the most significant period of severe weather during the event. ||The cold front stalled across the Mid-South on April 3rd while moist southwesterly flow aloft continued and anomalous precipitable water values prevailed across the region. Heavy rain fell across northern sections of the Mid-South into the morning hours of April 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 3-4 inches were common across east-central Arkansas, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi, along with scattered instances of flash flooding. By the afternoon of April 3rd, the airmass south of the stalled front destabilized and storms strengthened thanks to a 90-knot mid-level jet maximum moving through Missouri. These storms produced wind damage, large hail, flash flooding, and a tornado east of Corinth. Heavy rain continued into the early morning hours on April 4th when the front eventually lifted north of the area, resulting in a relative lull during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was uprooted across N Cummings Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat dome continued to strengthen and expand across the eastern CONUS during late June. The upper level pattern was dominated by a large 594 dam Bermuda High over the eastern US with a deep trough over the West Coast. This pattern persisted for several days before the ridge finally broke down and the westerlies advanced further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system moved out of central Arkansas and into the Mid-South during the mid-morning hours on June 7, 2025. The system re-intensified as it moved into an airmass that was favorable for severe storms. Moderate to strong instability, moderately steep mid-level lapse rates, and 0-6 km bulk shear values more than 40 kts helped support severe storms resulting in damaging winds, and a weak tornado. Storms cleared out of the Mid-South by early afternoon, and the atmosphere was able to recover and become unstable once again. An upper-level disturbance moving through during the evening hours produced another round of storms, some of them severe, with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down on CR 415 and Pebble Creek Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Underpass at Cass Street was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a shortwave slowly marched across the Mid-South, aided by diurnal instability a few strong to severe storms were able to grow and produce large hail and some wind damage and knock down trees and powerlines. Peak intensity of a storm occurred over Lafayette County where widespread damage occurred.

A heat dome continued to strengthen and expand across the eastern CONUS during late June. The upper level pattern was dominated by a large 594 dam Bermuda High over the eastern US with a deep trough over the West Coast. This pattern persisted for several days before the ridge finally broke down and the westerlies advanced further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 106 degrees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS reported a maximum heat index of 107 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system moved out of central Arkansas and into the Mid-South during the mid-morning hours on June 7, 2025. The system re-intensified as it moved into an airmass that was favorable for severe storms. Moderate to strong instability, moderately steep mid-level lapse rates, and 0-6 km bulk shear values more than 40 kts helped support severe storms resulting in damaging winds, and a weak tornado. Storms cleared out of the Mid-South by early afternoon, and the atmosphere was able to recover and become unstable once again. An upper-level disturbance moving through during the evening hours produced another round of storms, some of them severe, with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down in Hatley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another hot and humid day for the Mid-South. A stationary cold front was situated just north of the AR/MO and TN/KY state lines, which trapped the region on the hot and humid side of the boundary. Afternoon heat indices ranged from 105 to 109 degrees.  A shortwave was able to muster up enough shower and thunderstorm coverage over eastern portions of the state to have limited oppressive heat conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 108 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 23, 2025, a dangerous combination of oppressive heat and humidity gripped southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi, driving heat indices into the 113���115��F range. The synoptic setup shown in the surface analysis depicts a weak frontal boundary stalled well to the north, leaving the Gulf Coast entrenched in a hot, moist air mass with southerly surface flow. At 500 mb, broad ridging and subsidence extended over the region, suppressing vertical motion and allowing intense insolation to dominate. This upper-level pattern effectively ���capped in��� the heat, preventing relief from convection while enhancing compressional warming. Together, the stagnant surface boundary, Gulf moisture advection, and mid-level ridge created the perfect environment for extreme heat stress, leading to widespread excessive heat conditions across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of heat and humidity allowed heat indices to climb into the 113-115 degree range on July 23rd, which meet extreme heat conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 23, 2025, a dangerous combination of oppressive heat and humidity gripped southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi, driving heat indices into the 113���115��F range. The synoptic setup shown in the surface analysis depicts a weak frontal boundary stalled well to the north, leaving the Gulf Coast entrenched in a hot, moist air mass with southerly surface flow. At 500 mb, broad ridging and subsidence extended over the region, suppressing vertical motion and allowing intense insolation to dominate. This upper-level pattern effectively ���capped in��� the heat, preventing relief from convection while enhancing compressional warming. Together, the stagnant surface boundary, Gulf moisture advection, and mid-level ridge created the perfect environment for extreme heat stress, leading to widespread excessive heat conditions across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of heat and humidity allowed heat indices to climb into the 113-115 degree range on July 23rd, which meet extreme heat conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms impacted the Mississippi Gulf Coast and adjacent waters on July 1, 2025, as a cold front moved into a hot and humid airmass. Over land, storms produced damaging wind gusts up to 76 mph at Gulfport-Biloxi International Airport, resulting in downed trees, large branches, and power lines across Harrison County. Heavy rainfall also caused flash flooding in Harrison and Jackson Counties, with apartment units flooded in Gulfport, several roads covered in water, and stalled vehicles reported. Over the Mississippi Sound, thunderstorms generated measured wind gusts between 41 and 48 mph at Ship Island, Gulfport, and Bay Waveland Yacht Club buoys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple apartment units flooded by overflowing drainage ditch at Bellemont Gardens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms impacted the Mississippi Gulf Coast and adjacent waters on July 1, 2025, as a cold front moved into a hot and humid airmass. Over land, storms produced damaging wind gusts up to 76 mph at Gulfport-Biloxi International Airport, resulting in downed trees, large branches, and power lines across Harrison County. Heavy rainfall also caused flash flooding in Harrison and Jackson Counties, with apartment units flooded in Gulfport, several roads covered in water, and stalled vehicles reported. Over the Mississippi Sound, thunderstorms generated measured wind gusts between 41 and 48 mph at Ship Island, Gulfport, and Bay Waveland Yacht Club buoys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media showed video of car stalled due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 28th, another day of extreme heat gripped southeast Louisiana and coastal Mississippi, with heat indices once again soaring into the 113���115�� range. Surface analysis showed a sprawling ridge of high pressure centered over the Gulf and extending northward, suppressing convection and allowing oppressive heat and humidity to build unchecked. At the mid-levels, 500 mb heights climbed to near 594���599 dm across the region, signaling a strong, stagnant ridge overhead. This ridge trapped moisture near the surface while limiting vertical mixing, creating a ���heat dome��� effect that magnified dangerous heat stress. With little in the way of frontal passages or organized lift, conditions remained static through the afternoon, prolonging the exposure to extreme heat indices and exacerbating impacts for vulnerable populations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of heat and humidity allowed heat indices to climb into the 113-115 degree range on July 28th, which meet extreme heat conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 29th, 2025, excessive heat developed across southeast Louisiana and coastal Mississippi, where heat indices surged into the 113���115��F range. The surface analysis showed a weak pressure gradient and high dewpoints pooled across the northern Gulf Coast beneath a sprawling ridge aloft. At 500 mb, broad ridging with heights near 594���596 dm dominated the southern U.S., suppressing convection and reinforcing subsidence, which enhanced compressional heating. This combination of oppressive humidity and stagnant upper-level support allowed dangerous heat stress conditions to persist throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of heat and humidity allowed heat indices to climb into the 113-115 degree range on July 29th, which meet extreme heat conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms impacted the Mississippi Gulf Coast and adjacent waters on July 1, 2025, as a cold front moved into a hot and humid airmass. Over land, storms produced damaging wind gusts up to 76 mph at Gulfport-Biloxi International Airport, resulting in downed trees, large branches, and power lines across Harrison County. Heavy rainfall also caused flash flooding in Harrison and Jackson Counties, with apartment units flooded in Gulfport, several roads covered in water, and stalled vehicles reported. Over the Mississippi Sound, thunderstorms generated measured wind gusts between 41 and 48 mph at Ship Island, Gulfport, and Bay Waveland Yacht Club buoys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KGPT Gulfport-Biloxi Intl Airport measured a gust of 76mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 29th, 2025, excessive heat developed across southeast Louisiana and coastal Mississippi, where heat indices surged into the 113���115��F range. The surface analysis showed a weak pressure gradient and high dewpoints pooled across the northern Gulf Coast beneath a sprawling ridge aloft. At 500 mb, broad ridging with heights near 594���596 dm dominated the southern U.S., suppressing convection and reinforcing subsidence, which enhanced compressional heating. This combination of oppressive humidity and stagnant upper-level support allowed dangerous heat stress conditions to persist throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of heat and humidity allowed heat indices to climb into the 113-115 degree range on July 29th, which meet extreme heat conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms impacted the coastal waters and adjacent land areas of southeast Louisiana and Mississippi on July 16, 2025. Marine observations recorded multiple strong wind gusts, including 44 to 48 mph near Venice, 39 mph south of Sand Island, and 41 to 51 mph from Gulfport to Waveland, with the strongest gust along Beach Boulevard in Waveland. Over land, thunderstorm winds downed a tree in Waveland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A comment on broadcast media social media post indicated that a tree was downed by  thunderstorm winds in Waveland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave kicked off as a strong ridge moved into the Lower Mississippi Valley in response to a Bermuda High expanding into the southeastern United States. Excessive humidity and temperatures in the mid to upper 90s generated heat indices in excess of 105 degrees, with the Mississippi Delta Region suffering from Extreme Heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 109 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dangerous heat and humidity remained in place across eastern Arkansas as deep, tropical moisture continued to flow into the region. Dewpoints in the mid to upper 70s and temperatures in the low to mid 90s resulted in heat indices from 105 to 110 degrees. Convective coverage occurred across eastern portions of Mississippi, providing relief from oppressive heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi on July 29, 2025, along a weak boundary. In Pearl River County, MS, numerous trees were snapped or uprooted near Caesar, with a house losing shingles, an outbuilding destroyed, and several large pecan trees downed. Additional damage was reported in Hammond, LA, where thunderstorm winds downed a tree along Old Covington Highway. Over the waters, the SHBL1 station on Lake Borgne measured a wind gust of 51 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees snapped and uprooted around Caesar, MS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A primed microburst environment was in place on July 30, 2025, as hot and humid conditions combined with a weak surface low pressure across southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi. Numerous severe thunderstorms developed through the afternoon. In Marrero, Jefferson Parish, thunderstorm winds knocked down a powerline, while additional wind damage in Hammond brought down multiple trees, including one onto a powerline. In Centreville, MS, a downed tree damaged two homes. Heavy rainfall also led to flash flooding, with high water reported in Gretna, Baton Rouge, and Ascension Parish where parking lots were submerged and a vehicle partially underwater. Hail up to the size of quarters was observed in Baton Rouge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed tree damaged two homes in Centreville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, stationary heat dome brought oppressive heat to the Mid-South in mid to late August. The region was fed by warm, moist air from the Gulf, which elevated dewpoints and thus made heat-index values much higher than the actual air temperature alone. Because cloud cover was suppressed and the ground surface was dry, nighttime cooling was limited, elevating heat stress. The heat dome remained parked for several days, resulting in minimal forecast change or relief. Finally, an upper-level disturbance was able to bring relief as showers and thunderstorm chances returned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 108 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A structure fire caused by lightning was reported on CR 265.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Erin dominated the upper-level pattern and steered the remnants of an MCS directly towards the Mid-South. Shower and thunderstorm coverage was much higher than in previous days as storms tapped into moderate to high instability. Storms were able to quickly grow upscale and produce damaging winds and some sub-severe hail. Several outflow boundaries were produced from convective coverage with measured 40-50 mph winds associated with the boundaries. These winds were enough to produce sporadic damage in addition to stronger winds from severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple trees downed on Itasca Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Erin dominated the upper-level pattern and steered the remnants of an MCS directly towards the Mid-South. Shower and thunderstorm coverage was much higher than in previous days as storms tapped into moderate to high instability. Storms were able to quickly grow upscale and produce damaging winds and some sub-severe hail. Several outflow boundaries were produced from convective coverage with measured 40-50 mph winds associated with the boundaries. These winds were enough to produce sporadic damage in addition to stronger winds from severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed and power poles snapped on Peyton Road in the Poagville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were snapped associated with the rear flank downdraft of the Thaxton/New Albany tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, stationary heat dome brought oppressive heat to the Mid-South in mid to late August. The region was fed by warm, moist air from the Gulf, which elevated dewpoints and thus made heat-index values much higher than the actual air temperature alone. Because cloud cover was suppressed and the ground surface was dry, nighttime cooling was limited, elevating heat stress. The heat dome remained parked for several days, resulting in minimal forecast change or relief. Finally, an upper-level disturbance was able to bring relief as showers and thunderstorm chances returned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep, upper-level trough pushed multiple shortwaves towards the Mid-South during this time frame. Ahead of the main trough and embedded shortwaves, moisture surged into the region. High temperatures in the 90s combined with the increased moisture and limited convective coverage resulted in heat indices above 105 degrees during the afternoon hours each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 107 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant multi-hazard, multi-day event occurred across the Mid-South from April 2, 2025, to April 8, 2025, producing 35 tornadoes, record flooding, and numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail.  A large upper-level trough covered the Western U.S. in early April. A significant piece of energy rotated around the base of the trough and ejected into the Southern Plains and the Middle-Upper Mississippi Valley on April 2nd. A 500 mb jet maximum of 120 knots and a 300 mb jet maximum of 140 knots pushed into Iowa by late afternoon on April 2nd.  Meanwhile, a 992 mb surface low moved into the Upper Mississippi Valley with a trailing cold front pushing toward the Mid-South. A secondary, weaker surface low developed over northeast Arkansas and helped to back surface winds. The warm sector across the Mid-South was potent with surface dewpoints climbing into the upper 60s and MLCAPE values climbing to 2000-3000 J/kg. Increasing upper-level divergence occurred in the entrance region of the upper jet, resulting in strong lift across the Mid-South and storm initiation in the increasingly moist and unstable airmass. Hodographs were long, strongly curved, and supportive of tornadoes. Discrete supercells intensified across the Mid-South during the evening as 0-6 km bulk shear values increased to 60-70 knots, and 0-1 km helicity values increased to 300-400 m2/s2. Fifteen tornadoes occurred from late afternoon on April 2nd through about 2 am on April 3rd, including four EF-3s. This period represented the most significant period of severe weather during the event. ||The cold front stalled across the Mid-South on April 3rd while moist southwesterly flow aloft continued and anomalous precipitable water values prevailed across the region. Heavy rain fell across northern sections of the Mid-South into the morning hours of April 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 3-4 inches were common across east-central Arkansas, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi, along with scattered instances of flash flooding. By the afternoon of April 3rd, the airmass south of the stalled front destabilized and storms strengthened thanks to a 90-knot mid-level jet maximum moving through Missouri. These storms produced wind damage, large hail, flash flooding, and a tornado east of Corinth. Heavy rain continued into the early morning hours on April 4th when the front eventually lifted north of the area, resulting in a relative lull during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail along hwy 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of below-normal rainfall led to the development of severe drought (D2) conditions across Harrison and Jackson Counties. Rainfall deficits peaked during mid-October, with locations observing approximately 2.96 inches of rainfall since September 1 compared to a normal of 8.23 inches, a deficit of 5.27 inches. Severe drought conditions persisted through late October before widespread beneficial rainfall of 2 to 5 inches on October 25th and 26th improved soil moisture and hydrologic conditions, resulting in drought severity falling below D2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of below normal rainfall lead to severe drought in Harrison and Jackson Counties in Mississippi. These drought conditions would last from the start of the month until October 28th when beneficial rainfall lowered drought conditions below severe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October 2025 saw some improvement in drought conditions across north Mississippi, driven mainly by slightly above-average rainfall, especially during the final week of October. Severe Drought (D2) and Moderate Drought (D1) conditions persisted throughout the entire month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought (D2) conditions persisted through the entire month of October and into November.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down along Eagle Bend Road south of Yazoo City where the top of a Pecan tree was downed onto a home. Fortunately, the home sustained limited if any damage. The tornado continued to move northeast, parallel to Eagle Bend Road while staying slightly north of the roadway. Intermittent vegetative damage was noted with a isolated tree fall as well. It dissipated near Old Highway 3 where minor roofing tin peeling was noted. The tornado's maximum winds were estimated at 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November precipitation was below average with rainfall being sparse in the first three weeks of the month, with most falling later on. The limited November rain slowed or partially offset improvements achieved by rainfall in October, allowing Severe Drought (D2) conditions to gradually shrink in size, but continue to persist into December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) Drought conditions persisted in eastern Panola county through November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early in September, soil moisture was well below normal, and river flows were below typical values due to the lack of beneficial rainfall. Severe (D2) Drought conditions expanded and persisted through the month. Pastures were providing very little or no feed, and supplemental feeding was required to maintain livestock. Unfortunately, the seasonal outlook did not forecast improvement across north Mississippi, and drought conditions continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) Drought conditions were introduced in early September. Lack of beneficial rainfall resulted in no improvement and D2 conditions persisted into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the afternoon hours of June 11th in east Texas. These storms grew upscale and became a mescoscale convective system. Eastward movement of this MCS was driven by both generally westward flow aloft as well as cold pool outflow on the east side of it. Although the complex had lost much of its intensity by the time it reached southeast Louisiana, a portion of if was still able to produce severe winds southwest Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees down and power pole snapped in Centreville on Gordon Street from thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on New Light Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed over Mississippi on September 16th and 17th and produced hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down on the east side of Crystal Springs in the vicinity of MS Highway 27 and Lee Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Summertime convection developed in a regime of high moisture throughout the atmospheric column as well as high instability. One storm was able to reach severe limits and produced a downburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fence was downed by thunderstorm winds at Henrys Bar And Grill. There was alos wind damage to storage building at Atlas Auto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal Mississippi entered a prolonged period of suppressed rainfall leading to the development of D2 drought conditions by late September. Annual accumulated rainfall across the local area was near normal through mid-August. The precipitation pattern then dramatically changed from that point through the end of September. Only about 2 inches of rain fell during this 6-week period, compared to the 10 inches typically expected. The main driver of this dry spell was persistent upper level ridging aloft across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 drought conditions began to develop from lack of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft regime continued to be in place on June 9th with an upper level ridge center west of the local forecast area. This pattern carried developing storms from NW to SE across the area late in the afternoon. Sufficient instability aided in thunderstorm enhancement which resulted in severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed near South Swan Rd and Swan Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across north Mississippi during the early morning hours of September 15, 2025. Weak shear and marginal instability limited the strength of the storms. One strong storm caused some minor damage in Panola County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds caused minor damage to the roof covering at the abandoned Crenshaw  Elementary School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance moving through weak northwest flow over the Mid-South kicked off isolated showers and thunderstorms across north Mississippi during the evening hours of September 15, 2025. Enough instability was present to help a couple of storms intensify to severe limits and produce damaging winds.

An upper-level disturbance moving through weak northwest flow over the Mid-South kicked off isolated showers and thunderstorms across north Mississippi during the evening hours of September 15, 2025. Enough instability was present to help a couple of storms intensify to severe limits and produce damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were knocked down in the Byhalia area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lead shortwave on the southern periphery of a larger trough over the Central Plains was able to provide enough forcing for storms to form across east-central Arkansas during the early morning hours of September 19, 2025. These storms moved across northwest Mississippi during the morning, producing outflow boundaries that eventually resulted in new storm development over northeast Mississippi. These storms produced some damaging winds near Tupelo by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were knocked down across Tupelo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon heating combined with modest instability resulted in isolated to scattered thunderstorms across north Mississippi by late afternoon on September 21, 2025. Weak shear resulted in pulse-type storms, a few of which were strong with gusty winds.  One such storm damaged the roof of a gymnasium in Walls, MS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst removed the roof membrane from the gymnasium at Walls Elementary School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level disturbance moving through zonal flow aloft helped to trigger showers and thunderstorms across the Mid-South during the afternoon of September 21, 2025. Modest instability and weak shear resulted in mainly pulse-type storms with a couple of reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down on Old Hwy 7 South. A car struck the downed tree.  Power lines were knocked down on Hwy 7 North and Hwy 311.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and a power pole were blown down along Hargrave Road and US Highway 61.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north/south oriented trough axis extended from the Great Lakes to the Florida Panhandle and a ridge was centered over the Desert Southwest encompasses the rest of the country. That put the CWA right between both features. The frontal boundary associated with the trough stalled along the northern Gulf Coast. That front was the initial focal point for convection to begin developing early in the afternoon. That activity was been able to capitalize on very high instability and one storm produced hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Picture of hail estimated to be quarter sized west of Mcneil.
